metadata.title: The Velvet Straitjacket: Breaking Free from Corporate AI Voice
metadata.color_semantics: claude fidelity: INK #3D3929; SPARK #D97757 one accent.
metadata.author: Nik Bear Brown
metadata.credit_line: Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI

metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Many brands now sound like ChatGPT, and their customers can tell. Human writing has texture, brief asides, and small risks that machines smooth away. The efficient default voice is polished but reliably puts an audience to sleep. Consumers have a cultural radar for AI-slop because it always wears the same linguistic outfit.

artifactLines: Many brands now sound like ChatGPT, and their customers can tell.
Human writing has texture, brief asides, and small risks that machines smooth away.
The efficient default voice is polished but reliably puts an audience to sleep.
Consumers have a cultural radar for AI-slop because it always wears the same linguistic outfit.

beats.narration_text: The claim that my brand sounds like ChatGPT and customers can tell is uncomfortably plausible, so I want to fix it deliberately. Help me break out of the default voice. Show me: (1) the specific linguistic tics that make up the identical 'AI-slop' outfit readers now recognize, (2) how texture, brief asides, and small risks restore a human signal in writing, and (3) a before-and-after rewrite of one corporate paragraph so I can feel exactly what changes when the straitjacket comes off.

output: Everything is a testament to our unwavering commitment to seamles
Nobody is blaming you for using generative AI
